title: Core Features
description: A deep dive into ten essential features of modern web development, from rendering strategies and API design to accessibility, performance optimization, and state management patterns.

blocks.heading: Core Features
blocks.body: Modern web applications share a common set of features that users expect. This section explains ten of the most important ones, each with real code examples and practical guidance. Understanding these features will prepare you to build production-quality applications.

blocks.title: 1. Rendering Strategies: SSR vs CSR vs SSG

blocks.body: How your page gets built and delivered to the browser is one of the most important architectural decisions in web development. There are three main strategies, each with different trade-offs for performance, SEO, and developer experience.

blocks.heading: Client-Side Rendering (CSR)
blocks.body: With CSR, the server sends a nearly empty HTML page with a JavaScript bundle. The browser downloads the JavaScript, runs it, and builds the page in the user's browser. This is how a plain React app (created with Vite or Create React App) works by default.

blocks.description: The minimal HTML shell served in a client-side rendered app

blocks.heading: Server-Side Rendering (SSR)
blocks.body: With SSR, the server runs your React components on every request and sends fully-formed HTML to the browser. The user sees content immediately, and then React "hydrates" the page to make it interactive. Next.js uses SSR by default for pages that need fresh data.

blocks.description: A server-rendered page that fetches fresh data on every request

blocks.heading: Static Site Generation (SSG)
blocks.body: With SSG, pages are built once at build time and served as static HTML files. This is the fastest option because there is no server computation per request -- the HTML is pre-built and can be served from a CDN. Ideal for content that does not change often, like blog posts or documentation.

blocks.description: A statically generated page with generateStaticParams

blocks.body: Next.js lets you mix strategies in the same app. A marketing page can be SSG, the dashboard can be SSR, and an interactive widget can be CSR -- all in one codebase.

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: CSR builds the page in the browser (slow first load, always fresh). SSR builds the page on the server per request (fast first load, great SEO). SSG pre-builds pages at build time (fastest, but data can be stale). Choose based on how often your data changes and whether SEO matters.

blocks.title: 2. API Routes and Backend for Frontend

blocks.body: API routes let you build backend endpoints directly inside your Next.js project. Instead of spinning up a separate Express server, you create handler files that respond to HTTP requests. This is the Backend for Frontend (BFF) pattern -- your frontend has its own lightweight backend layer.

blocks.heading: Creating an API route
blocks.body: In the Next.js App Router, API routes are created by placing a route.ts file inside the app/api/ directory. Each file exports functions named after HTTP methods.

blocks.description: API route with GET and POST handlers

blocks.heading: Dynamic API routes
blocks.body: Use bracket folders for dynamic segments, just like page routes.

blocks.description: Dynamic API route for single-resource operations

blocks.heading: Why use API routes instead of direct API calls?
blocks.body: API routes act as a proxy between your frontend and external services. They keep API keys secret, validate data before forwarding, and let you combine multiple external APIs into a single response.

blocks.description: API route that hides external API keys from the client

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: API routes let you build backend logic inside your Next.js project. They keep secrets safe, validate input, and simplify your frontend code by providing a clean internal API. Use them as a proxy layer between your UI and external services.

Internationalization means building your app so it can support multiple languages without changing any component code. Instead of hardcoding text like "Submit" into your buttons, you use a key like t('form.submit') that looks up the correct translation from a JSON file.

blocks.heading: How it works in Next.js with next-intl
blocks.body: The next-intl library provides three pieces: a [locale] folder for language routing, JSON translation files, and a useTranslations hook for components.

blocks.title: Create translation files
blocks.body: Each language gets its own JSON file with the same keys but translated values.

blocks.title: Configure supported locales
blocks.body: Define which languages your app supports and which is the default.

blocks.title: Use translations in components
blocks.body: Call useTranslations with a namespace to access the translated strings.

blocks.heading: Handling plurals and interpolation
blocks.body: Translations often need dynamic values or plural forms. next-intl handles both with ICU message syntax.

blocks.description: Translation keys with interpolation and plurals
Passing variables to translation strings

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Internationalization separates text from code. Put all user-facing strings in JSON files keyed by language. Use useTranslations to look up the correct string at render time. Adding a new language is just creating a new JSON file -- no component changes needed.

Dark mode is no longer a nice-to-have -- users expect it. A proper theming system lets you define colors, typography, and spacing in one place and swap the entire palette with a single state change. This section shows how to build it with Material UI and Redux.

blocks.heading: Defining a theme factory
blocks.body: A theme factory function takes a mode parameter and returns a complete theme object. Every color, shadow, and component override is defined once.

blocks.description: Theme factory that produces light or dark MUI themes

blocks.heading: Connecting theme to Redux state
blocks.body: Store the current mode in a Redux slice. A ThemeRegistry component reads the mode and wraps the app with the correct theme.

blocks.description: Redux slice for theme mode with toggle action
ThemeRegistry reads Redux state and provides the MUI theme

blocks.heading: Respecting system preferences
blocks.body: You can detect the user's OS-level dark mode preference with a media query and use it as the initial value.

blocks.description: Using matchMedia to detect OS-level dark mode setting

blocks.heading: Theme toggle flow
items: Redux updates mode from 'light' to 'dark' (or vice versa)
ThemeRegistry reads the new mode and rebuilds the theme with useMemo
MUI re-renders every component with the new color palette
Redux Persist saves the preference to localStorage for next visit

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Define your entire color palette in a theme factory function. Store the mode in Redux so any component can toggle it. Use CssBaseline to apply theme colors globally. Detect system preferences with matchMedia and persist the user's choice with Redux Persist.

A Progressive Web App is a website that can behave like a native app. Users can install it on their home screen, use it offline, and receive push notifications. PWAs bridge the gap between web and native without requiring app store distribution.

blocks.heading: The Web App Manifest
blocks.body: The manifest is a JSON file that tells the browser how your app should appear when installed. It defines the app name, icons, theme color, and display mode.

blocks.description: Web app manifest for installable PWA

blocks.heading: Service Workers for offline support
blocks.body: A Service Worker is a script that runs in the background, separate from your page. It intercepts network requests and can serve cached responses when the user is offline.

blocks.description: Basic Service Worker with cache-first strategy

blocks.heading: Registering the Service Worker
blocks.body: Register the Service Worker in your app's entry point so the browser knows to install it.

blocks.description: Register the Service Worker on page load

cards.title: Installable
cards.body: Users can add the app to their home screen and launch it like a native app

cards.title: Offline Ready
cards.body: Service Workers cache assets so the app works without an internet connection

cards.title: Push Notifications
cards.body: Send notifications to re-engage users even when the app is closed

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: PWAs need three things: HTTPS, a web app manifest, and a Service Worker. The manifest makes the app installable. The Service Worker enables offline support and caching. Together they give your web app a native-like experience without an app store.

Performance directly impacts user experience and search rankings. A page that takes five seconds to load loses nearly half its visitors. This section covers the three most impactful optimization techniques: code splitting, lazy loading, and image optimization.

blocks.heading: Code splitting with dynamic imports
blocks.body: Instead of sending all your JavaScript in one giant bundle, code splitting breaks it into smaller chunks that load on demand. Users only download the code they actually need.

blocks.description: Lazy-loading a heavy component only when it is needed

blocks.heading: Lazy loading images
blocks.body: Images are typically the heaviest assets on a page. Lazy loading defers loading off-screen images until the user scrolls to them.

blocks.description: Next.js Image component with automatic optimization

blocks.heading: Bundle analysis and tree shaking
blocks.body: Tree shaking removes unused code from your bundle. Import only what you need from libraries to keep bundles small.

blocks.description: Importing the entire lodash library (70+ KB)

tabs.label: Good: imports only what is needed

blocks.description: Importing only the specific function needed (~2 KB)

blocks.heading: Performance optimization checklist
rows: High -- automatic format, sizing, lazy loading
Import specific functions, not entire libraries
Medium -- can save 50+ KB per library
Medium -- shows instant feedback during navigation
Use React.memo for expensive list items
Medium -- reduces API calls and re-renders

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: The biggest performance wins come from sending less code (code splitting, tree shaking) and loading assets on demand (lazy loading images and components). Use next/image for automatic image optimization. Import specific functions instead of entire libraries.

SEO determines whether people can find your site through search engines. Good SEO means providing structured metadata, generating sitemaps, and ensuring search engine crawlers can read your content. Next.js has built-in support for all of these.

blocks.heading: Metadata with the Metadata API
blocks.body: Next.js App Router lets you define metadata by exporting a metadata object or a generateMetadata function from any page or layout.

blocks.description: Dynamic metadata generated from page content

blocks.heading: Generating a sitemap
blocks.body: A sitemap tells search engines which pages exist on your site and how often they change.

blocks.description: Dynamic sitemap generation for all pages

blocks.heading: Structured data with JSON-LD
blocks.body: Structured data helps search engines understand your content. It can result in rich snippets -- enhanced search results with ratings, prices, or FAQ dropdowns.

blocks.description: JSON-LD structured data for a blog post

blocks.heading: SEO checklist
items: Every page has a unique title and meta description
Open Graph and Twitter Card meta tags are set for social sharing
A sitemap.xml is generated and submitted to search engines
Images have descriptive alt text for accessibility and image search
Use SSR or SSG so search engines see fully-rendered HTML
Add JSON-LD structured data for rich search snippets
Create a robots.txt file to guide crawler behavior

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: SEO starts with metadata: title, description, and Open Graph tags. Use generateMetadata for dynamic pages. Generate a sitemap so search engines discover all your pages. Add JSON-LD structured data for rich snippets. Use SSR or SSG so crawlers see real content, not empty HTML.

Accessibility means making your website usable by everyone, including people who use screen readers, keyboard-only navigation, or have visual impairments. The Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) define the standards. Accessibility is not optional -- it is a legal requirement in many countries and makes your site better for all users.

blocks.heading: Semantic HTML
blocks.body: The most impactful accessibility improvement is using the right HTML elements. Screen readers understand semantic elements like nav, main, button, and heading levels. They cannot interpret div and span.

blocks.description: Inaccessible layout using only divs
blocks.code:
// BAD: Screen readers cannot understand this structure
Home
About
Welcome
Hello world

blocks.description: Accessible layout using semantic elements
blocks.code:
// GOOD: Screen readers understand the page structure
Home About

Welcome

Hello world

blocks.heading: Keyboard navigation
blocks.body: Every interactive element must be reachable and operable with a keyboard. Users should be able to Tab through focusable elements, press Enter or Space to activate them, and press Escape to close modals.

blocks.description: A modal that traps focus and responds to Escape

blocks.heading: ARIA attributes
blocks.body: ARIA (Accessible Rich Internet Applications) attributes provide extra information to screen readers when semantic HTML alone is not enough.

blocks.description: Essential ARIA attributes for interactive components

blocks.heading: WCAG quick reference
rows: Users can see or hear all content
Alt text on images, captions on video, sufficient color contrast
Users can interact with all controls
Keyboard navigation, no time limits, skip navigation links
Clear language, consistent navigation, helpful error messages
Valid HTML, proper ARIA, semantic elements

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Accessibility starts with semantic HTML: use the right element for the job. Ensure all interactive elements work with keyboard. Use ARIA attributes when HTML alone is not enough. Test with a screen reader (VoiceOver on Mac, NVDA on Windows) and a keyboard.

State management is how you store, share, and update data in your application. Choosing the wrong approach leads to props being passed through dozens of components (prop drilling), unnecessary re-renders, or state that gets out of sync. There is no single best solution -- the right choice depends on the scope and nature of your data.

blocks.heading: Local state with useState
blocks.body: Use useState for data that belongs to a single component and does not need to be shared. Form inputs, toggle states, and UI-only data are good candidates.

blocks.description: useState for component-local UI state

blocks.heading: useReducer for complex local state
blocks.body: When a component has multiple pieces of related state that change together, useReducer makes the logic clearer by centralizing all transitions in a single function.

blocks.description: useReducer for complex form state with multiple fields

blocks.heading: React Context for shared stable data
blocks.body: React Context is best for data that many components need but that changes infrequently. The current locale, the authenticated user, or a form validation context are ideal use cases.

blocks.description: Context for sharing auth state across the app

blocks.heading: Redux for global, frequently-changing state
blocks.body: When state needs to be shared across unrelated components and changes frequently, Redux provides predictable updates with a single source of truth. Examples: theme mode, shopping cart, notification queue.

blocks.heading: Which approach to use
rows: Data needs to be shared across siblings or distant components
Forms with validation, state machines, multiple related values
Simple boolean toggles or single values
Auth, locale, theme (data that changes rarely)
High-frequency updates (causes entire subtree to re-render)
Theme, cart, notifications, data that persists across pages
Data used by only one component

blocks.title: Common Mistake
blocks.body: Do not put everything in Redux. Most state is local. If a piece of state is only used by one component, useState is the right choice. Only promote state to context or Redux when multiple unrelated components need it.

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Start with useState. Upgrade to useReducer when transitions get complex. Use Context for widely-shared stable data. Use Redux for global state that changes often and should persist. The right tool depends on how widely the state is shared and how frequently it changes.

Forms are the primary way users send data to your application. Good form handling means validating input before submission, showing clear error messages, managing multi-step flows, and providing instant feedback. This section covers patterns used in production forms.

blocks.heading: Controlled inputs and real-time validation
blocks.body: In React, controlled inputs store their value in state. This gives you full control over validation, formatting, and submission.

blocks.description: Form with real-time field validation and error messages

blocks.heading: Multi-step forms
blocks.body: Complex forms like checkout flows or onboarding wizards are split into multiple steps. Each step is a component, and a parent manages which step is active and collects data across steps.

blocks.description: Multi-step form with progress indicator, step validation, and collected data

blocks.heading: Debouncing input for API-backed validation
blocks.body: Some validations require a server call -- for example, checking if a username is already taken. Debouncing ensures you do not fire an API request on every keystroke.

blocks.description: Custom hook for debouncing API-backed validation

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Use controlled inputs for full validation control. Show errors only after the field is touched (onBlur). Split complex forms into steps with a shared data object. Debounce API-backed validations to avoid excessive requests. Always pair labels with inputs and use aria-invalid for accessibility.

keyPoints: CSR, SSR, and SSG are the three rendering strategies -- choose based on data freshness needs and SEO requirements
API routes act as a Backend for Frontend, keeping API keys secret and simplifying client code
Internationalization separates text from components using translation JSON files and the useTranslations hook
Dark mode requires a theme factory function, a Redux slice for the mode, and a ThemeRegistry provider component
PWAs need three things: HTTPS, a web app manifest for installability, and a Service Worker for offline support
The biggest performance wins come from code splitting, lazy loading images, and importing only what you need
SEO depends on metadata, sitemaps, structured data, and server-rendered HTML that crawlers can read
Accessibility starts with semantic HTML, keyboard navigation, focus management, and ARIA attributes
State management goes from local (useState) to complex local (useReducer) to shared (Context) to global (Redux)
Form handling combines controlled inputs, real-time validation, multi-step flows, and debounced API checks
prerequisites: Basic understanding of React components, props, and state
Familiarity with JavaScript ES6 features and async/await
